text: And he answered her: Thou knowest that Esau, my brother, is a hairy man, and I am smooth:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If my father should feel me, and perceive it, I fear lest he will think I would have mocked him, and I shall bring upon me a curse instead of a blessing.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And his mother said to him: Upon me be this curse, my son: only hear thou my voice, and go, fetch me the things which I have said.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He went, and brought, and gave them to his mother. She dressed meats, such as she knew his father liked.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And she put on him very good garments of Esau, which she had at home with her:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the little skins of the kids she put about his hands, and covered the bare of his neck.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And she gave him the savoury meat, and delivered him bread that she had baked.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Which when he had carried in, he said: My father? But he answered: I hear. Who art thou, my son?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Jacob said: I am Esau, thy firstborn: I have done as thou didst command me: arise, sit and eat of my venison, that thy soul may bless me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Isaac said to his son: How couldst thou find it so quickly, my son? He answered: It was the will of God, that what I sought came quickly in my way:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Isaac said: Come hither, that I may feel thee, my son, and may prove whether thou be my son Esau, or no.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He came near to his father, and when he had felt him, Isaac said: The voice indeed is the voice of Jacob; but the hands, are the hands of Esau.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he knew him not, because his hairy hands made him like to the elder. Then blessing him,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He said: Art thou my son Esau? He answered: I am.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then he said: Bring me the meats of thy hunting, my son, that my soul may bless thee. And when they were brought, and he had eaten, he offered him wine also, which after he had drunk,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He said to him: Come near me, and give me a kiss, my son.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He came near, and kissed him. And immediately as he smelled the fragrant smell of his garments, blessing him, he said: Behold, the smell of my son is as the smell of a plentiful field, which the Lord hath blessed.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: God give thee of the dew of heaven, and of the fatness of the earth, abundance of corn and wine.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And let peoples serve thee, and tribes worship thee: be thou lord of thy brethren, and let thy mother’s children bow down before thee. Cursed be he that curseth thee: and let him that blesseth thee be filled with blessings.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Isaac had scarce ended his words, when, Jacob being now gone out abroad, Esau came,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And brought in to his father meats, made of what he had taken in hunting, saying: Arise, my father, and eat of thy son’s venison; that thy soul may bless me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Isaac said to him: Why! who art thou? He answered: I am thy firstborn son, Esau.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Isaac was struck with fear, and astonished exceedingly; and wondering beyond what can be believed, said: Who is he then that even now brought me venison that he had taken, and I ate of all before thou camest? and I have blessed him, and he shall be blessed.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Esau having heard his father’s words, roared out with a great cry; and, being in a consternation, said: Bless me also, my father.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said: Thy brother came deceitfully and got thy blessing.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But he said again: Rightly is his name called Jacob; for he hath supplanted me lo this second time: My birthright he took away before, and now this second time he hath stolen away my blessing. And again he said to his father: Hast thou not reserved me also a blessing?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Isaac answered: I have appointed him thy lord, and have made all his brethren his servants: I have established him with corn and wine, and after this, what shall I do more for thee, my son?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Esau said to him: Hast thou only one blessing, father? I beseech thee bless me also. And when he wept with a loud cry,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Isaac being moved, said to him: In the fat of the earth, and in the dew of heaven from above,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Shall thy blessing be. Thou shalt live by the sword, and shalt serve thy brother: and the time shall come, when thou shalt shake off and loose his yoke from thy neck.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Esau therefore always hated Jacob, for the blessing wherewith his father had blessed him; and he said in his heart: The days will come of the mourning for my father, and I will kill my brother Jacob.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: These things were told to Rebecca: and she sent and called Jacob, her son, and said to him: Behold Esau, thy brother, threateneth to kill thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now therefore, my son, hear my voice, arise and flee to Laban, my brother, to Haran:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And thou shalt dwell with him a few days, till the wrath of thy brother be assuaged,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And his indignation cease, and he forget the things thou hast done to him: afterwards I will send, and bring thee from thence hither. Why shall I be deprived of both my sons in one day?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Rebecca said to Isaac: I am weary of my life, because of the daughters of Heth: if Jacob take a wife of the stock of this land, I choose not to live.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Isaac called Jacob, and blessed him, and charged him, saying: Take not a wife of the stock of Chanaan:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But go, and take a journey to Mesopotamia of Syria, to the house of Bathuel, thy mother’s father, and take thee a wife thence of the daughters of Laban, thy uncle.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And God almighty bless thee, and make thee to increase and multiply thee: that thou mayst be a multitude of people.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And give the blessings of Araham to thee, and to thy seed after thee: that thou mayst possess the land of thy sojournment, which he promised to thy grandfather.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when Isaac had sent him away, he took his journey and went to Mesopotamia of Syria, to Laban, the son of Bathuel, the Syrian, brother to Rebecca, his mother.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Esau seeing that his father had blessed Jacob, and had sent him into Mesopotamia of Syria, to marry a wife thence; and that after the blessing he had charged him, saying: Thou shalt not take a wife of the daughters of Chanaan:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And that Jacob obeying his parents, was gone into Syria:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Experiencing also, that his father was not well pleased with the daughters of Chanaan:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He went to Ismael, and took to wife, besides them he had before, Maheleth, the daughter of Ismael, Abraham’s son, the sister of Nabajoth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Jacob being departed from Bersabee, went on to Haran.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when he was come to a certain place, and would rest in it after sunset, he took of the stones that lay there, and putting under his head, slept in the same place.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he saw in his sleep a ladder standing upon the earth, and the top thereof touching heaven: the angels also of God ascending and descending by it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord leaning upon the ladder saying to him: I am the Lord God of Abraham thy father, and the God of Isaac: The land, wherein thou sleepest, I will give to thee and to thy seed.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And thy seed shall be as the dust of the earth: thou shalt spread abroad to the west, and to the east, and to the north, and to the south: and IN THEE and thy seed, all the tribes of the earth SHALL BE BLESSED.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And I will be thy keeper whithersoever thou goest, and will bring thee back into this land: neither will I leave thee, till I shall have accomplished all that I have said.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when Jacob awaked out of sleep, he said: Indeed the Lord is in this place, and I knew it not.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And trembling, he said: How terrible is this place? this is no other but the house of God, and the gate of heaven.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Jacob arising in the morning, took the stone which he had laid under his head, and set it up for a title, pouring oil upon the top of it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he called the name of the city Bethel, which before was called Luza.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he made a vow, saying: If God shall be with me, and shall keep me in the way, by which I walk, and shall give me bread to eat, and raiment to put on,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And I shall return prosperously to my father’s house: the Lord shall be my God:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And this stone, which I have set up for a title, shall be called the house of God: and of all things that thou shalt give to me, I will offer tithes to thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Jacob went on in his journey, and came into the east country.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he saw a well in the field, and three flocks of sheep lying by it: for the beasts were watered out of it, and the mouth thereof was closed with a great stone.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the custom was, when all the sheep were gathered together, to roll away the stone, and after the sheep were watered, to put it on the mouth of the well again.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said to the shepherds: Brethren, whence are you? They answered: Of Haran.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he asked them, saying: Know you Laban, the son of Nachor? They said: We know him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He said: Is he in health? He is in health, say they: and behold, Rachel, his daughter, cometh with his flock.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Jacob said: There is yet much day remaining, neither is it time to bring the flocks into the folds again: first give the sheep drink, and so lead them back to feed.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They answered: We cannot, till all the cattle be gathered together, and we remove the stone from the well’s mouth, that we may water the flocks.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They were yet speaking, and behold Rachel came with her father’s sheep; for she fed the flock.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when Jacob saw her, and knew her to be his cousin german, and that they were the sheep of Laban, his uncle: he removed the stone wherewith the well was closed.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And having watered the flock, he kissed her: and lifting up his voice wept.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he told her that he was her father’s brother, and the son of Rebecca: but she went in haste and told her father.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who, when he heard that Jacob his sister’s son was come, ran forth to meet him: and embracing him, and heartily kissing him, brought him into his house. And when he had heard the causes of his journey,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He answered: Thou art my bone and my flesh. And after the days of one month were expired,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He said to him: Because thou art my brother, shalt thou serve me without wages? Tell me what wages thou wilt have.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now he had two daughters, the name of the elder was Lia; and the younger was called Rachel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Lia was blear-eyed: Rachel was well favoured, and of a beautiful countenance.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Jacob being in love with her, said: I will serve thee seven years for Rachel, thy younger daughter.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Laban answered: It is better that I give her to thee than to another man; stay with me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So Jacob served seven years for Rachel: and they seemed but a few days, because of the greatness of his love.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said to Laban: Give me my wife; for now the time is fulfilled, that I may go in unto her.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he, having invited a great number of his friends to the feast, made the marriage.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And at night he brought in Lia, his daughter, to him,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Giving his daughter a handmaid, named Zelpha. Now when Jacob had gone in to her according to custom, when morning was come he saw it was Lia.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said to his father-in-law: What is it that thou didst mean to do? did not I serve thee for Rachel? why hast thou deceived me?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Laban answered: It is not the custom in this place, to give the younger in marriage first.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Make up the week of days of this match: and I will give thee her also, for the service that thou shalt render me other seven years.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He yielded to his pleasure: and after the week was past, he married Rachel:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: To whom her father gave Bala, for her servant.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And having at length obtained the marriage he wished for, he preferred the love of the latter before the former, and served with him other seven years.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord seeing that he despised Lia, opened her womb, but her sister remained barren.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And she conceived and bore a son, and called his name Ruben, saying: The Lord saw my affliction: now my husband will love me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And again she conceived and bore a son, and said: Because the Lord heard that I was despised, he hath given this also to me: and she called his name Simeon.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And she conceived the third time, and bore another son, and said: Now also my husband will be joined to me, because I have borne him three sons: and therefore she called his name Levi.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The fourth time she conceived and bore a son, and said: Now will I praise the Lord: and for this she called him Juda. And she left bearing.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Rachel seeing herself without children, envied her sister, and said to her husband: Give me children, otherwise I shall die.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Jacob being angry with her, answered: Am I as God, who hath deprived thee of the fruit of thy womb?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But she said: I have here my servant Bala: go in unto her, that she may bear upon my knees, and I may have children by her.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And she gave him Bala in marriage: who,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When her husband had gone in unto her, conceived and bore a son.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Rachel said: The Lord hath judged for me, and hath heard my voice, giving me a son; and therefore she called his name Dan.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And again Bala conceived, and bore another,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For whom Rachel said: God hath compared me with my sister, and I have prevailed: and she called him Nephthali.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Lia perceiving that she had left of bearing, gave Zelpha, her handmaid, to her husband.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when she had conceived, and brought forth a son,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She said: Happily. And therefore called his name Gad.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Zelpha also bore another.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Lia said: This is for my happiness: for women will call me blessed. Therefore she called him Aser.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Ruben going out in the time of the wheat harvest into the field, found mandrakes: which he brought to his mother Lia. And Rachel said: Give me part of thy son’s mandrakes.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She answered: Dost thou think it a small matter, that thou hast taken my husband from me, unless thou take also my son’s mandrakes? Rachel said: He shall sleep with thee this night, for thy son’s mandrakes.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when Jacob returned at even from the field, Lia went out to meet him, and said: Thou shalt come in unto me, because I have hired thee for my son’s mandrakes. And he slept with her that night.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And God heard her prayers; and she conceived: and bore a fifth son:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And said: God hath given me a reward, because I gave my handmaid to my husband. And she called his name Issachar.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Lia conceived again, and bore the sixth son,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And said: God hath endowed me with a good dowry; this turn also my husband will be with me, because I have borne him six sons: and therefore she called his name Zabulon.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: After whom she bore a daughter, named Dina.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The Lord also remembering Rachel, heard her, and opened her womb.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And she conceived, and bore a son, saying: God hath taken away my reproach.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And she called his name Joseph: saying: The Lord give me also another son.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when Joseph was born, Jacob said to his father-in-law: Send me away, that I may return into my country, and to my land.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Give me my wives, and my children, for whom I have served thee, that I may depart: thou knowest the service that I have rendered thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Laban said to him: Let me find favour in thy sight: I have learned, by experience, that God hath blessed me for thy sake.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Appoint thy wages which I shall give thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But he answered: Thou knowest how I have served thee, and how great thy possession hath been in my hands.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou hadst but little before I came to thee, and now thou art become rich: and the Lord hath blessed thee at my coming. It is reasonable, therefore, that I should now provide also for my own house.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Laban said: What shall I give thee? But he said: I require nothing; but if thou wilt do what I demand, I will feed and keep thy sheep again.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Go round through all thy flocks, and separate all the sheep of divers colours, and speckled; and all that is brown and spotted, and of divers colours, as well among the sheep as among the goats, shall be my wages.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And my justice shall answer for me tomorrow before thee, when the time of the bargain shall come; and all that is not of divers colours, and spotted, and brown, as well among the sheep as among the goats, shall accuse me of theft.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Laban said: I like well what thou demandest.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he separated the same day the she-goats, and the sheep, and the he-goats, and the rams of divers colours, and spotted; and all the flock of one colour, that is, of white and black fleece, he delivered into the hands of his sons.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he set the space of three days journey betwixt himself and his son-in-law, who fed the rest of his flock.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Jacob took green rods of poplar, and of almond, and of plane-trees, and pilled them in part: so when the bark was taken off, in the parts that were pilled, there appeared whiteness: but the parts that were whole, remained green: and by this means the colour was divers.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he put them in the troughs, where the water was poured out; that when the flocks should come to drink, they might have the rods before their eyes, and in the sight of them might conceive.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And it came to pass, that in the very heat of coition, the sheep beheld the rods, and brought forth spotted, and of divers colours, and speckled.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Jacob separated the flock, and put the rods in the troughs before the eyes of the rams; and all the white and the black were Laban’s, and the rest were Jacob’s, when the flocks were separated one from the other.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So when the ewes went first to ram, Jacob put the rods in the troughs of water before the eyes of the rams, and of the ewes, that they might conceive while they were looking upon them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But when the later coming was, and the last conceiving, he did not put them. And those that were lateward, became Laban’s; and they of the first time, Jacob’s.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the man was enriched exceedingly, and he had many flocks, maid-servants and men-servants, camels and asses.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But after that he had heard the words of the sons of Laban, saying: Jacob hath taken away all that was our father’s, and being enriched by his substance is become great.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And perceiving also, that Laban’s countenance was not towards him as yesterday and the other day.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Especially the Lord saying to him: Return into the land of thy fathers and to thy kindred, and I will be with thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He sent, and called Rachel and Lia into the field, where he fed the flocks,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And said to them: I see your father’s countenance is not towards me as yesterday and the other day: but the God of my father hath been with me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And you know that I have served your father to the uttermost of my power.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Yea your father hath also overreached me, and hath changed my wages ten times: and yet God hath not suffered him to hurt me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If at any time, he said: The speckled shall be thy wages: all the sheep brought forth speckled: but when he said on the contrary: Thou shalt take all the white one for thy wages: all the flocks brought forth white ones.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And God hath taken your father’s substance, and given it to me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For after the time came of the ewes conceiving, I lifted up my eyes, and saw in my sleep, that the males which leaped upon the females were of divers colours, and spotted, and speckled.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the angel of God said to me in my sleep: Jacob. And I answered: Here I am.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said: Lift up thy eyes, and see that all the males leaping upon the females, are of divers colours, spotted and speckled. For I have seen all that Laban hath done to thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I am the God of Bethel, where thou didst anoint the stone, and make a vow to me. Now therefore arise, and go out of this land, and return into thy native country.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Rachel and Lia answered: Have we any thing left among the goods and inheritance of our father’s house?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Hath he not counted us as strangers, and sold us, and eaten up the price of us?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But God hath taken our father’s riches, and delivered them to us, and to our children: wherefore, do all that God hath commanded thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Jacob rose up, and having set his children and wives upon camels, went his way.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he took all his substance, and flocks, and whatsoever he had gotten in Mesopotamia, and went forward to Isaac, his father, to the land of Chanaan.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: At that time Laban was gone to shear his sheep, and Rachel stole away her father’s idols.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Jacob would not confess to his father-in-law that he was flying away.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when he was gone, together with all that belonged to him, and having passed the river, was going on towards mount Galaad,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: It was told Laban on the third day, that Jacob fled.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he took his brethren with him, and pursued after him seven days; and overtook him in the mount of Galaad.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he saw in a dream God, saying to him: Take heed thou speak not any thing harshly against Jacob.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now Jacob had pitched his tent in the mountain: and when he, with his brethren, had overtaken him, he pitched his tent in the same mount of Galaad.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said to Jacob: Why hast thou done thus, to carry away, without my knowledge, my daughters as captives taken with the sword?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Why wouldst thou run away privately, and not acquaint me, that I might have brought thee on the way with joy, and with songs, and with timbrels, and with harps?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou hast not suffered me to kiss my sons and daughters; thou hast done foolishly; and now indeed,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: It is in my power to return thee evil; but the God of your father said to me yesterday: Take heed thou speak not any thing harshly against Jacob.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Suppose thou didst desire to go to thy friends, and hadst a longing after thy father’s house: why hast thou stolen away my gods?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Jacob answered: That I departed unknown to thee, it was for fear lest thou wouldst take away thy daughters by force.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But, whereas, thou chargest me with theft: with whomsoever thou shalt find thy gods, let him be slain before our brethren. Search, and if thou find any of thy things with me, take them away. Now when he said this, he knew not that Rachel had stolen the idols.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So Laban went into the tent of Jacob, and of Lia, and of both the handmaids, and found them not. And when he was entered into Rachel’s tent,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She, in haste, hid the idols under the camel’s furniture, and sat upon them: and when he had searched all the tent, and found nothing,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She said: Let not my lord be angry that I cannot rise up before thee, because it has now happened to me according to the custom of women. So his careful search was in vain.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Jacob being angry, said in a chiding manner: For what fault of mine, and for what offence on my part hast thou so hotly pursued me,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And searched all my household stuff? What hast thou found of all the substance of thy house? lay it here before my brethren, and thy brethren, and let them judge between me and thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Have I, therefore, been with thee twenty years? thy ewes and goats were not barren, the rams of thy flocks I did not eat:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Neither did I shew thee that which the beast had torn; I made good all the damage: whatsoever was lost by theft, thou didst exact it of me:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Day and night was I parched with heat, and with frost, and sleep departed from my eyes.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And in this manner have I served thee in thy house twenty years, fourteen for thy daughters, and six for thy flocks: thou hast changed also my wages ten times.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Unless the God of my father, Abraham, and the fear of Isaac, had stood by me, peradventure now thou hadst sent me away naked: God beheld my affliction and the labour of my hands, and rebuked thee yesterday.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Laban answered him: The daughters are mine, and the children, and thy flocks, and all things that thou seest are mine: what can I do to my children, and grandchildren?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Come, therefore, let us enter into a league; that it may be for a testimony between me and thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Jacob took a stone, and set it up for a title.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said to his brethren: Bring hither stones. And they, gathering stones together, made a heap, and they ate upon it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Laban called it, The witness heap; and Jacob, The hillock of testimony: each of them according to the propriety of his language.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Laban said: This heap shall be a witness between me and thee this day, and therefore the name thereof was called Galaad, that is, The witness heap.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The Lord behold and judge between us, when we shall be gone one from the other.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If thou afflict my daughters, and if thou bring in other wives over them: none is witness of our speech but God, who is present and beholdeth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said again to Jacob: Behold this heap, and the stone which I have set up between me and thee,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Shall be a witness: this heap, I say, and the stone, be they for a testimony, if either I shall pass beyond it going towards thee, or thou shalt pass beyond it thinking harm to me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The God of Abraham, and the God of Nachor, the God of their father, judge between us. And Jacob swore by the fear of his father Isaac:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And after he had offered sacrifices in the mountain, he called his brethren to eat bread. And when they had eaten, they lodged there:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Laban arose in the night, and kissed his sons and daughters, and blessed them: and returned to his place.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Jacob also went on the journey he had begun: and the angels of God met him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when he saw them, he said: These are the camps of God, and he called the name of that place Mahanaim, that is, Camps.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he sent messengers before him to Esau, his brother, to the land of Seir, to the country of Edom:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he commanded them, saying: Thus shall ye speak to my lord Esau: Thus saith thy brother Jacob: I have sojourned with Laban, and have been with him until this day:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I have oxen, and asses, and sheep, and menservants, and womenservants: and now I send a message to my lord, that I may find favour in thy sight.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the messengers returned to Jacob, saying: We came to Esau, thy brother, and behold he cometh with speed to meet thee with four hundred men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Jacob was greatly afraid; and in his fear divided the people that was with him, and the flocks, and the sheep, and the oxen, and the camels, into two companies,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Saying: If Esau come to one company, and destroy it, the other company that is left, shall escape.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Jacob said: O God of my father Abraham, and God of my father Isaac: O Lord who saidst to me, Return to thy land, and to the place of thy birth, and I will do well for thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I am not worthy of the least of all thy mercies, and of thy truth which thou hast fulfilled to thy servant. With my staff I passed over this Jordan; and now I return with two companies.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Deliver me from the hand of my brother Esau, for I am greatly afraid of him; lest perhaps he come, and kill the mother with the children.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou didst say, that thou wouldst do well by me, and multiply my seed like the sand of the sea, which cannot be numbered for multitude.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when he had slept there that night, he set apart, of the things which he had, presents for his brother Esau,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Two hundred she-goats, twenty he-goats, two hundred ewes, and twenty rams,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thirty milch camels with their colts, forty kine, and twenty bulls, twenty she-asses, and ten of their foals.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he sent them by the hands of his servants, every drove by itself, and he said to his servants: Go before me, and let there be a space between drove and drove.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he commanded the first, saying: If thou meet my brother Esau, and he ask thee: Whose art thou? or whither goest thou? or whose are these before thee?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou shalt answer: Thy servant Jacob’s: he hath sent them as a present to my lord Esau; and he cometh after us.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In like manner he commanded the second, and the third, and all that followed the droves, saying: Speak ye the same words to Esau, when ye find him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And ye shall add: Thy servant Jacob himself also followeth after us; for he said: I will appease him with the presents that go before, and afterwards I will see him, perhaps he will be gracious to me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So the presents went before him, but himself lodged that night in the camp.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And rising early, he took his two wives and his two handmaids, with his eleven sons, and passed over the ford of Jaboc.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when all things were brought over that belonged to him,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He remained alone; and behold, a man wrestled with him till morning.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when he saw that he could not overcome him, he touched the sinew of his thigh, and forthwith it shrank.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said to him: Let me go, for it is break of day. He answered: I will not let thee go, except thou bless me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said: What is thy name? He answered: Jacob.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But he said: Thy name shall not be called Jacob, but Israel; for if thou hast been strong against God, how much more shalt thou prevail against men?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Jacob asked him: Tell me by what name art thou called? He answered: Why dost thou ask my name? And he blessed him in the same place.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Jacob called the name of the place Phanuel, saying: I have seen God face to face, and my soul has been saved.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And immediately the sun rose upon him, after he was past Phanuel; but he halted on his foot.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Therefore the children of Israel, unto this day, eat not the sinew, that shrank in Jacob’s thigh: because he touched the sinew of his thigh and it shrank.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Jacob lifting up his eyes, saw Esau coming, and with him four hundred men: and he divided the children of Lia and of Rachel, and of the two handmaids.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he put both the handmaids and their children foremost: and Lia and her children in the second place: and Rachel and Joseph last.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he went forward and bowed down with his face to the ground seven times, until his brother came near.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Esau ran to meet his brother, and embraced him: and clasping him fast about the neck, and kissing him, wept.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And lifting up his eyes, he saw the women and their children, and said: What mean these? And do they belong to thee? He answered: They are the children which God hath given to me, thy servant.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then the handmaids and their children came near and bowed themselves.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Lia also, with her children, came near and bowed down in like manner; and last of all, Joseph and Rachel bowed down.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Esau said: What are the droves that I met? He answered: That I might find favour before my lord.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But he said: I have plenty, my brother, keep what is thine for thyself.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Jacob said: Do not so I beseech thee, but if I have found favour in thy eyes, receive a little present at my hands: for I have seen thy face, as if I should have seen the countenance of God: be gracious to me,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And take the blessing which I have brought thee, and which God hath given me, who giveth all things. He took it with much ado at his brother’s earnest pressing him,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And said: Let us go on together, and I will accompany thee in thy journey.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Jacob said: My lord, thou knowest that I have with me tender children, and sheep, and kine with young: which if I should cause to be overdriven, in one day all the flocks will die.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: May it please my lord to go before his servant: and I will follow softly after him, as I shall see my children to be able, until I come to my lord in Seir.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Esau answered: I beseech thee, that some of the people, at least, who are with me, may stay to accompany thee in the way. And he said: There is no necessity: I want nothing else but only to find favour, my lord, in thy sight.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So Esau returned that day, the way that he came, to Seir.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Jacob came to Socoth: where having built a house, and pitched tents, he called the name of the place Socoth, that is, Tents.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he passed over to Salem, a city of the Sichemites, which is in the land of Chanaan, after he returned from Mesopotamia of Syria: and he dwelt by the town.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he bought that part of the field, in which he pitched his tents, of the children of Hemor, the father of Sichem, for a hundred lambs.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And raising an altar there, he invoked upon it the most mighty God of Israel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Dina the daughter of Lia went out to see the women of that country.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when Sichem the son of Hemor the Hevite, the prince of that land, saw her, he was in love with her: and took her away, and lay with her, ravishing the virgin.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And his soul was fast knit unto her; and whereas she was sad, he comforted her with sweet words.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And going to Hemor his father, he said: Get me this damsel to wife.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But when Jacob had heard this, his sons being absent, and employed in feeding the cattle, he held his peace till they came back.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when Hemor the father of Sichem was come out to speak to Jacob,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Behold his sons came from the field: and hearing what had passed, they were exceeding angry, because he had done a foul thing in Israel, and committed an unlawful act, in ravishing Jacob’s daughter.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Hemor spoke to them: The soul of my son Sichem has a longing for your daughter: give her him to wife:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And let us contract marriages one with another: give us your daughters, and take you our daughters.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And dwell with us: the land is at your command, till, trade, and possess it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Sichem also said to her father and to her brethren: Let me find favour in your sight, and whatsoever you shall appoint I will give:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Raise the dowry, and ask gifts, and I will gladly give what you shall demand: only give me this damsel to wife.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The sons of Jacob answered Sichem and his father deceitfully, being enraged at the deflowering of their sister:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: We cannot do what you demand, nor give our sister to one that is uncircumcised; which with us is unlawful and abominable.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But in this we may be allied with you, if you will be like us, and all the male sex among you be circumcised:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then will we mutually give and take your daughters, and ours; and we will dwell with you, and will be one people:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But if you will not be circumcised, we will take our daughter and depart.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Their offer pleased Hemor, and Sichem, his son:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the young man made no delay, but forthwith fulfilled what was required: for he loved the damsel exceedingly, and he was the greatest man in all his father’s house.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And going into the gate of the city, they spoke to the people:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: These men are peaceable, and are willing to dwell with us: let them trade in the land, and till it, which being large and wide wanteth men to till it: we shall take their daughters for wives, and we will give them ours.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: One thing there is for which so great a good is deferred: We must circumcise every male among us, following the manner of the nation.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And their substance, and cattle, and all that they possess, shall be ours; only in this let us condescend, and by dwelling together, we shall make one people.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they all agreed, and circumcised all the males.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And behold the third day, when the pain of the wound was greatest: two of the sons of Jacob, Simeon and Levi, the brothers of Dina, taking their swords, entered boldly into the city and slew all the men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they killed also Hemor and Sichem, and took away their sister Dina out of Sichem’s house.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when they were gone out, the other sons of Jacob came upon the slain; and plundered the city in revenge of the rape.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they took their sheep, and their herds, and their asses, wasting all they had in their houses and in their fields.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And their children and wives they took captive.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when they had boldly perpetrated these things, Jacob said to Simeon and Levi: You have troubled me, and made me hateful to the Chanaanites and Pherezites, the inhabitants of this land. We are few: they will gather themselves together and kill me; and both I, and my house shall be destroyed.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They answered: Should they abuse our sister as a strumpet?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In the mean time God said to Jacob: Arise and go up to Bethel, and dwell there, and make there an altar to God, who appeared to thee when thou didst flee from Esau, thy brother.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Jacob having called together all his household, said: Cast away the strange gods that are among you, and be cleansed, and change your garments.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Arise, and let us go up to Bethel, that we may make there an altar to God; who heard me in the day of my affliction, and accompained me in my journey.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So they gave him all the strange gods they had, and the earrings which were in their ears: and he buried them under the turpentine tree, that is behind the city of Sichem.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when they were departed, the terror of God fell upon all the cities round about, and they durst not pursue after them as they went away.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Jacob came to Luza, which is in the land of Chanaan, surnamed Bethel: he and all the people that were with him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he built there an altar, and called the name of that place, The house of God: for there God appeared to him when he fled from his brother.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: At the same time Debora, the nurse of Rebecca, died, and was buried at the foot of Bethel, under an oak, and the name of that place was called, The oak of weeping.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And God appeared again to Jacob, after he returned from Mesopotamia of Syria, and he blessed him,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Saying: Thou shalt not be called any more Jacob, but Israel shall be thy name. And he called him Israel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And said to him: I am God almighty, increase thou and be multiplied. Nations and peoples of nations shall be from thee, and kings shall come out of thy loins.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the land which I gave to Abraham and Isaac, I will give to thee, and to thy seed after thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he departed from him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But he set up a monument of stone, in the place where God had spoken to him: pouring drink-offerings upon it, and pouring oil thereon:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And calling the name of that place Bethel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And going forth from thence, he came in the spring time to the land which leadeth to Ephrata: wherein when Rachel was in travail,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: By reason of her hard labour, she began to be in danger, and the midwife said to her: Fear not, for thou shalt have this son also.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when her soul was departing for pain, and death was now at hand, she called the name of her son Benoni, that is, the son of my pain: but his father called him Benjamin, that is, the son of the right hand.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So Rachel died, and was buried in the highway that leadeth to Ephrata, this is Bethlehem.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Jacob erected a pillar over her sepulchre: this is the pillar of Rachel’s monument, to this day.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Departing thence, he pitched his tent beyond the Flock tower.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when he dwelt in that country, Ruben went, and slept with Bala the concubine of his father: which he was not ignorant of. Now the sons of Jacob were twelve.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The sons of Lia: Ruben the first born, and Simeon, and Levi, and Juda, and Issachar, and Zabulon.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The sons of Rachel: Joseph and Benjamin.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The sons of Bala, Rachel’s handmaid: Dan and Nephthali.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The sons of Zelpha, Lia’s handmaid: Gad and Aser: these are the sons of Jacob, that were born to him in Mesopotamia of Syria.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he came to Isaac his father in Mambre, the city of Arbee, this is Hebron: wherein Abraham and Isaac sojourned.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the days of Isaac were a hundred and eighty years.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And being spent with age he died, and was gathered to his people, being old and full of days: and his sons Esau and Jacob buried him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And these are the generations of Esau, the same is Edom.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Esau took wives of the daughters of Chanaan: Ada the daughter of Elon the Hethite, and Oolibama the daughter of Ana, the daughter of Sebeon the Hevite:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Basemath, the daughter of Ismael, sister of Nabajoth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Ada bore Eliphaz: Basemath bore Rahuel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Oolibama bore Jehus, and Ihelon, and Core. These are the sons of Esau, that were born to him in the land of Chanaan.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Esau took his wives, and his sons and daughters, and every soul of his house, and his substance, and cattle, and all that he was able to acquire in the land of Chanaan: and went into another country, and departed from his brother Jacob.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For they were exceeding rich, and could not dwell together: neither was the land in which they sojourned able to bear them, for the multitude of their flocks.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Esau dwelt in mount Seir: he is Edom.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And these are the generations of Esau, the father of Edom, in mount Seir.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And these the names of his sons: Eliphaz the son of Ada, the wife of Esau: and Rahuel, the son of Basemath, his wife.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Eliphaz had sons: Theman, Omar, Sepho, and Gatham and Cenez.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Thamna was the concubine of Eliphaz, the son of Esau: and she bore him Amalech. These are the sons of Ada, the wife of Esau.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the sons of Rahuel were Nahath and Zara, Samma and Meza. These were the sons of Basemath, the wife of Esau.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And these were the sons of Oolibama, the daughter of Ana, the daughter of Sebeon, the wife of Esau, whom she bore to him, Jehus, and Ihelon, and Core.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: These were dukes of the sons of Esau: the sons of Eliphaz, the firstborn of Esau: duke Theman, duke Omar, duke Sepho, duke Cenez,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Duke Core, duke Gatham, duke Amalech: these are the sons of Eliphaz, in the land of Edom, and these the sons of Ada.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And these were the sons of Rahuel, the son of Esau: duke Nahath, duke Zara, duke Samma, duke Meza. And these are the dukes of Rahuel, in the land of Edom: these the sons of Basemath, the wife of Esau.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And these the sons of Oolibama, the wife of Esau: duke Jehus, duke Ihelon, duke Core. These are the dukes of Oolibama, the daughter of Ana, and wife of Esau.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: These are the sons of Esau, and these the dukes of them: the same is Edom.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: These are the sons of Seir, the Horrite, the inhabitants of the land: Lotan, and Sobal, and Sebeon, and Ana,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Dison, and Eser, and Disan. These are dukes of the Horrites, the sons of Seir, in the land of Edom.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Lotan had sons: Hori and Heman. And the sister of Lotan was Thamna.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And these the sons of Sobal: Alvan, and Manahat, and Ebal, and Sepho, and Onam.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And these the sons of Sebeon: Aia and Ana. This is Ana that found the hot waters in the wilderness, when he fed the asses of Sebeon, his father:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he had a son Dison, and a daughter Oolibama.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And these were the sons of Dison: Hamdan, and Eseban, and Jethram, and Charan.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: These also were the sons of Eser: Balaan, and Zavan, and Acan.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Dison had sons: Hus and Aram.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: These were dukes of the Horrites: duke Lotan, duke Sobal, duke Sebeon, duke Ana,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Duke Dison, duke Eser, duke Disan: these were dukes of the Horrites that ruled in the land of Seir.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the kings that ruled in the land of Edom, before the children of Israel had a king, were these:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Bela the son of Beor, and the name of his city Denaba.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Bela died, and Jobab, the son of Zara, of Bosra, reigned in his stead.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when Jobab was dead, Husam, of the land of the Themanites, reigned in his stead.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And after his death, Adad, the son of Badad, reigned in his stead, who defeated the Madianites in the country of Moab; and the name of his city was Avith.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when Adad was dead, there reigned in his stead, Semla, of Masreca.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he being dead, Saul, of the river Rohoboth, reigned in his stead.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when he also was dead, Balanan, the son of Achobor, succeeded to the kingdom.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: This man also being dead, Adar reigned in his place; and the name of his city was Phau: and his wife was called Meetabel, the daughter of Matred, daughter of Mezaab.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And these are the names of the dukes of Esau in their kindreds, and places, and callings: duke Thamna, duke Alva, duke Jetheth,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Duke Oolibama, duke Ela, duke Phinon,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Duke Cenez, duke Theman, duke Mabsar,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Duke Magdiel, duke Hiram: these are the dukes of Edom dwelling in the land of their government; the same is Esau, the father of the Edomites.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Jacob dwelt in the land of Chanaan, wherein his father sojourned.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And these are his generations: Joseph, when he was sixteen years old, was feeding the flock with his brethren, being but a boy: and he was with the sons of Bala and of Zelpha his father’s wives: and he accused his brethren to his father of a most wicked crime.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now Israel loved Joseph above all his sons, because he had him in his old age: and he made him a coat of divers colours.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And his brethren seeing that he was loved by his father, more than all his sons, hated him, and could not speak peaceably to him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now it fell out also that he told his brethren a dream, that he had dreamed: which occasioned them to hate him the more.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said to them: Hear my dream which I dreamed.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I thought we were binding sheaves in the field: and my sheaf arose as it were, and stood, and your sheaves standing about bowed down before my sheaf.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: His brethren answered: Shalt thou be our king? or shall we be subject to thy dominion? Therefore this matter of his dreams and words ministered nourishment to their envy and hatred.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He dreamed also another dream, which he told his brethren, saying: I saw in a dream, as it were the sun, and the moon, and eleven stars worshipping me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when he had told this to his father, and brethren, his father rebuked him and said: What meaneth this dream that thou hast dreamed? shall I and thy mother, and thy brethren worship thee upon the earth?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: His brethren therefore envied him: but his father considered the thing with himself.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when his brethren abode in Sechem, feeding their father’s flocks,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Israel said to him: Thy brethren feed the sheep in Sichem: come, I will send thee to them. And when he answered:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I am ready: he said to him: Go, and see if all things be well with thy brethren, and the cattle: and bring me word again what is doing. So being sent from the vale of Hebron, he came to Sichem:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And a man found him there wandering in the field, and asked what he sought.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But he answered: I seek my brethren, tell me where they feed the flocks.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the man said to him: They are departed from this place: for I heard them say: Let us go to Dothain. And Joseph went forward after his brethren, and found them in Dothain.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when they saw him afar off, before he came nigh them, they thought to kill him:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And said one to another: Behold the dreamer cometh.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Come, let us kill him, and cast him into some old pit: and we will say: Some evil beast hath devoured him: and then it shall appear what his dreams avail him:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Ruben hearing this, endeavoured to deliver him out of their hands, and said:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner
