text: Do not take away his life, nor shed his blood: but cast him into this pit, that is in the wilderness, and keep your hands harmless: now he said this, being desirous to deliver him out of their hands and to restore him to his father.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And as soon as he came to his brethren, they forthwith stript him of his outside coat, that was of divers colours:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And cast him into an old pit where there was not water.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And sitting down to eat bread, they saw some Ismaelites on their way coming from Galaad, with their camels, carrying spices, and balm, and myrrh to Egypt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Juda said to his brethren: What will it profit us to kill our brother, and conceal his blood?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: It is better that he be sold to the Ismaelites, and that our hands be not defiled: for he is our brother and our flesh. His brethren agreed to his words.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when the Madianite merchants passed by, they drew him out of the pit, and sold him to the Ismaelites, for twenty pieces of silver: and they led him into Egypt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Ruben returning to the pit, found not the boy:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And rending his garments he went to his brethren, and said: The boy doth not appear, and whither shall I go?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they took his coat, and dipped it in the blood of a kid, which they had killed:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Sending some to carry it to their father, and to say: This we have found: see whether it be thy son’s coat, or not.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the father acknowledging it, said: It is my son’s coat, an evil wild beast hath eaten him, a beast hath devoured Joseph.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And tearing his garments, he put on sackcloth, mourning for his son a long time.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And all his children being gathered together to comfort their father in his sorrow, he would not receive comfort, but said: I will go down to my son into hell, mourning. And whilst he continued weeping,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The Madianites sold Joseph in Egypt to Putiphar, an eunuch of Pharao, captain of the soldiers.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: At that time Juda went down from his brethren, and turned in to a certain Odollamite, named Hiras.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he saw there the daughter of a man of Chanaan, called Sue: and taking her to wife, he went in unto her.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And she conceived, and bore a son, and called his name Her.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And conceiving again, she bore a son, and called him Onan.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She bore also a third: whom she called Sela. After whose birth, she ceased to bear any more.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Juda took a wife for Her, his first born, whose name was Thamar.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Her, the first born of Juda, was wicked in the sight of the Lord: and was slain by him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Juda, therefore, said to Onan his son: Go in to thy brother’s wife and marry her, that thou mayst raise seed to thy brother.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He knowing that the children should not be his, when he went in to his brother’s wife, he spilled his seed upon the ground, lest children should be born in his brother’s name.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And therefore the Lord slew him, because he did a detestable thing:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wherefore Juda said to Thamar his daughter-in-law: Remain a widow in thy father’s house, till Sela my son grow up: for he was afraid lest he also might die, as his brethren did. She went her way, and dwelt in her father’s house.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And after many days were past: the daughter of Sue the wife of Juda died: and when he had taken comfort after his mourning, he went up to Thamnas, to the shearers of his sheep, he and Hiras the Odollamite, the shepherd of his flock.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And it was told Thamar that her father-in-law was come up to Thamnas to shear his sheep.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And she put off the garments of her widowhood, and took a veil: and changing her dress, sat in the cross way, that leadeth to Thamnas: because Sela was grown up, and she had not been married to him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When Juda saw her, he thought she was a harlot: for she had covered her face, lest she should be known.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And going to her, he said: Suffer me to lie with thee: for he knew her not to be his daughter-in-law. And she answered: What wilt thou give me to enjoy my company?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He said: I will send thee a kid out of the flock. And when she said again: I will suffer what thou wilt, if thou give me a pledge, till thou send what thou promisest.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Juda said: What wilt thou have for a pledge? She answered: Thy ring and bracelet, and the staff which thou holdest in thy hand. The woman therefore at one copulation conceived.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And she arose and went her way: and putting off the apparel which she had taken, put on the garments of her widowhood.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Juda sent a kid by his shepherd, the Odollamite, that he might receive the pledge again, which he had given to the woman: but he, not finding her,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Asked the men of that place: Where is the woman that sat in the cross way? And when they all made answer: There was no harlot in this place,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He returned to Juda, and said to him: I have not found her; moreover, the men of that place said to me, that there never sat a harlot there.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Juda said: Let her take it to herself, surely she cannot charge us with a lie, I sent the kid which I promised: and thou didst not find her.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And behold, after three months, they told Juda, saying: Thamar, thy daughter-in-law, hath played the harlot, and she appeareth to have a big belly. And Juda said: Bring her out that she may be burnt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But when she was led to execution, she sent to her father in law, saying: By the man, to whom these things belong, I am with child. See whose ring, and bracelet, and staff this is.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But he acknowledging the gifts, said: She is juster than I: because I did not give her to Sela, my son. However he knew her no more.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when she was ready to be brought to bed, there appeared twins in her womb: and in the very delivery of the infants, one put forth a hand, whereon the midwife tied a scarlet thread, saying:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: This shall come forth the first.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But he drawing back his hand, the other came forth: and the woman said: Why is the partition divided for thee? and therefore called his name Phares.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Afterwards his brother came out, on whose hand was the scarlet thread: and she called his name Zara.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Joseph was brought into Egypt, and Putiphar, an eunuch of Pharao, chief captain of the army, an Egyptian, bought him of the Ismaelites, by whom he was brought.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord was with him, and he was a prosperous man in all things: and he dwelt in his master’s house:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who knew very well that the Lord was with him, and made all that he did to prosper in his hand.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Joseph found favour in the sight of his master, and ministered to him: and being set over all by him, he governed the house committed to him, and all things that were delivered to him:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord blessed the house of the Egyptian for Joseph’s sake, and multiplied all his substance, both at home and in the fields.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Neither knew he any other thing, but the bread which he ate. And Joseph was of a beautiful countenance, and comely to behold.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And after many days, his mistress cast her eyes on Joseph, and said: Lie with me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But he in no wise consenting to that wicked act said to her: Behold, my master hath delivered all things to me, and knoweth not what he hath in his own house:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Neither is there any thing which is not in my power, or that he hath not delivered to me, but thee, who art his wife; how then can I do this wicked thing, and sin against my God?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: With such words as these day by day, both the woman was importunate with the young man, and he refused the adultery.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now it happened on a certain day, that Joseph went into the house, and was doing some business, without any man with him:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And she catching the skirt of his garment, said: Lie with me. But he leaving the garment in her hand, fled, and went out.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when the woman saw the garment in her hands, and herself disregarded,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She called to her the men of her house, and said to them: See, he hath brought in a Hebrew, to abuse us: he came in to me, to lie with me; and when I cried out,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he heard my voice, he left the garment that I held, and got him out.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For a proof therefore of her fidelity, she kept the garment, and shewed it to her husband when he returned home:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And said: The Hebrew servant, whom thou hast brought, came to me to abuse me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when he heard me cry, he left the garment which I held, and fled out.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: His master hearing these things, and giving too much credit to his wife’s words, was very angry,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And cast Joseph into the prison, where the king’s prisoners were kept, and he was there shut up.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But the Lord was with Joseph, and having mercy upon him gave him favour in the sight of the chief keeper of the prison:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who delivered into his hand all the prisoners that were kept in custody: and whatsoever was done, was under him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Neither did he himself know any thing, having committed all things to him: for the Lord was with him, and made all that he did to prosper.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: After this, it came to pass, that two eunuchs, the butler and the baker of the king of Egypt, offended their lord.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Pharao being angry with them, (now the one was chief butler, the other chief baker,)

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He sent them to the prison of the commander of the soldiers, in which Joseph also was prisoner.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But the keeper of the prison delivered them to Joseph, and he served them. Some little time passed, and they were kept in custody.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they both dreamed a dream the same night, according to the interpretation agreeing to themselves:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when Joseph was come into them in the morning, and saw them sad,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He asked them, saying: Why is your countenance sadder today than usual?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They answered: We have dreamed a dream, and there is nobody to interpret it to us. And Joseph said to them: Doth not interpretation belong to God? Tell me what you have dreamed:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The chief butler first told his dream: I saw before me a vine,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: On which were three branches, which by little and little sent out buds, and after the blossoms brought forth ripe grapes:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the cup of Pharao was in my hand: and I took the grapes, and pressed them into the cup which I held, and I gave the cup to Pharao.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Joseph answered: This is the interpretation of the dream: The three branches, are yet three days:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: After which Pharao will remember thy service, and will restore thee to thy former place: and thou shalt present him the cup according to thy office, as before thou was wont to do.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Only remember me when it shall be well with thee, and do me this kindness: to put Pharao in mind to take me out of this prison:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For I was stolen away out of the land of the Hebrews, and here without any fault was cast into the dungeon.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The chief baker seeing that he had wisely interpreted the dream, said: I also dreamed a dream, That I had three baskets of meal upon my head:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And that in one basket which was uppermost, I carried all meats that are made by the art of baking, and that the birds ate out of it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Joseph answered: This is the interpretation of the dream: The three baskets, are yet three days:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: After which Pharao will take thy head from thee, and hang thee on a cross, and the birds shall tear thy flesh.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The third day after this was the birthday of Pharao: and he made a great feast for his servants, and at the banquet remembered the chief butler, and the chief baker.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he restored the one to his place, to present him the cup:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The other he hanged on a gibbet, that the truth of the interpreter might be shewn.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But the chief butler, when things prospered with him, forgot his interpreter.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: After two years Pharao had a dream. He thought he stood by the river,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Out of which came up seven kine, very beautiful and fat: and they fed in marshy places.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Other seven also came up out of the river, ill-favoured, and lean fleshed: and they fed on the very bank of the river, in green places:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they devoured them, whose bodies were very beautiful and well conditioned. So Pharao awoke.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He slept again, and dreamed another dream: Seven ears of corn came up upon one stalk full and fair:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then seven other ears sprung up thin and blasted,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And devoured all the beauty of the former. Pharao awaked after his rest:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when morning was come, being struck with fear, he sent to all the interpreters of Egypt, and to all the wise men: and they being called for, he told them his dream, and there was not any one that could interpret it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then at length the chief butler remembering, said: I confess my sin:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The king being angry with his servants, commanded me and the chief baker to be cast into the prison of the captain of the soldiers.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Where in one night both of us dreamed a dream foreboding things to come.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There was there a young man a Hebrew, servant to the same captain of the soldiers: to whom we told our dreams,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And we heard what afterwards the event of the thing proved to be so. For I was restored to my office: and he was hanged upon a gibbet.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Forthwith at the king’s command Joseph was brought out of the prison, and they shaved him: and changing his apparel brought him in to him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said to him: I have dreamed dreams, and there is no one that can expound them: Now I have heard that thou art very wise at interpreting them:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Joseph answered: Without me, God shall give Pharao a prosperous answer.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So Pharao told what he had dreamed: Methought I stood upon the bank of the river,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And seven kine came up out of the river, exceeding beautiful and full of flesh: and they grazed on green places in a marshy pasture.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And behold, there followed these, other seven kine, so very ill-favoured and lean, that I never saw the like in the land of Egypt:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they devoured and consumed the former,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And yet gave no mark of their being full: but were as lean and ill-favoured as before. I awoke, and then fell asleep again,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And dreamed a dream: Seven ears of corn grew up upon one stalk, full and very fair.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Other seven also thin and blasted, sprung of the stalk:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they devoured the beauty of the former: I told this dream to the conjecturers, and there is no man that can expound it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Joseph answered: The king’s dream is one: God hath shewn to Pharao what he is about to do.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The seven beautiful kine, and the seven full ears, are seven years of plenty: and both contain the same meaning of the dream.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the seven lean and thin kine that came up after them, and the seven thin ears that were blasted with the burning wind, are seven years of famine to come:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Which shall be fulfilled in this order.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Behold, there shall come seven years of great plenty in the whole land of Egypt:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: After which shall follow other seven years of so great scarcity, that all the abundance before shall be forgotten: for the famine shall consume all the land,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the greatness of the scarcity shall destroy the greatness of the plenty.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And for that thou didst see the second time a dream pertaining to the same thing: it is a token of the certainty, and that the word of God cometh to pass, and is fulfilled speedily.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now therefore let the king provide a wise and industrious man, and make him ruler over the land of Egypt:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That he may appoint overseers over all the countries: and gather into barns the fifth part of the fruits, during the seven fruitful years,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That shall now presently ensue: and let all the corn be laid up, under Pharao’s hands, and be reserved in the cities.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And let it be in readiness, against the famine of seven years to come, which shall oppress Egypt, and the land shall not be consumed with scarcity.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The counsel pleased Pharao, and all his servants.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said to them: Can we find such another man, that is full of the spirit of God?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He said therefore to Joseph: Seeing God hath shewn thee all that thou hast said, can I find one wiser and one like unto thee?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou shalt be over my house, and at the commandment of thy mouth all the people shall obey: only in the kingly throne will I be above thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And again Pharao said to Joseph: Behold, I have appointed thee over the whole land of Egypt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he took his ring from his own hand, and gave it into his hand: and he put upon him a robe of silk, and put a chain of gold about his neck.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he made him go up into his second chariot, the crier proclaiming that all should bow their knee before him, and that they should know he was made governor over the whole land of Egypt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king said to Joseph: I am Pharao: without thy commandment no man shall move hand or foot in all the land of Egypt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he turned his name, and called him in the Egyptian tongue the saviour of the world. And he gave him to wife Aseneth, the daughter of Putiphare, priest of Heliopolis. Then Joseph went out to the land of Egypt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: (Now he was thirty years old when he stood before king Pharao), and he went round all the countries of Egypt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the fruitfulness of the seven years came: and the corn being bound up into sheaves, was gathered together into the barns of Egypt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And all the abundance of grain was laid up in every city.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And there was so great abundance of wheat, that it was equal to the sand of the sea, and the plenty exceeded measure.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And before the famine came, Joseph had two sons born: whom Aseneth, the daughter of Putiphare, priest of Heliopolis, bore unto him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he called the name of the firstborn Manasses, saying: God hath made me to forget all my labours, and my father’s house.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he named the second Ephraim, saying: God hath made me to grow in the land of my poverty.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now when the seven years of plenty that had been in Egypt were passed:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The seven years of scarcity, which Joseph had foretold, began to come: and the famine prevailed in the whole world, but there was bread in all the land of Egypt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when there also they began to be famished, the people cried to Pharao, for food. And he said to them: Go to Joseph: and do all that he shall say to you.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the famine increased daily in all the land: and Joseph opened all the barns, and sold to the Egyptians: for the famine had oppressed them also.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And all provinces came into Egypt, to buy food, and to seek some relief of their want.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Jacob hearing that food was sold in Egypt, said to his sons: Why are ye careless?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I have heard that wheat is sold in Egypt: Go ye down, and buy us necessaries, that we may live, and not be consumed with want.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So the ten brethren of Joseph went down, to buy corn in Egypt:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Whilst Benjamin was kept at home by Jacob, who said to his brethren: Lest perhaps he take any harm in the journey.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they entered into the land of Egypt with others that went to buy. For the famine was in the land of Chanaan.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Joseph was governor in the land of Egypt, and corn was sold by his direction to the people. And when his brethren had bowed down to him,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he knew them, he spoke as it were to strangers, somewhat roughly, asking them: Whence came you? They answered: From the land of Chanaan, to buy necessaries of life.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And though he knew his brethren, he was not known by them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And remembering the dreams, which formerly he had dreamed, he said to them: You are spies. You are come to view the weaker parts of the land.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But they said: It is not so, my lord; but thy servants are come to buy food.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: We are all the sons of one man: we are come as peaceable men, neither do thy servants go about any evil.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he answered them: It is otherwise: you are come to consider the unfenced parts of this land.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But they said: We thy servants are twelve brethren, the sons of one man in the land of Chanaan: the youngest is with our father, the other is not living.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He saith, This is it that I said: You are spies.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I shall now presently try what you are: by the health of Pharao, you shall not depart hence, until your youngest brother come.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Send one of you to fetch him: and you shall be in prison, till what you have said be proved, whether it be true or false: or else by the health of Pharao you are spies.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So he put them in prison three days.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the third day he brought them out of prison, and said: Do as I have said, and you shall live: for I fear God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If you be peaceable men, let one of your brethren be bound in prison: and go ye your ways, and carry the corn that you have bought, unto your houses.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And bring your youngest brother to me, that I may find your words to be true, and you may not die. They did as he had said.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they talked one to another: We deserve to suffer these things, because we have sinned against our brother, seeing the anguish of his soul, when he besought us, and we would not hear: therefore is this affliction come upon us.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Ruben, one of them, said: Did not I say to you: Do not sin against the boy; and you would not hear me? Behold his blood is required.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they knew not that Joseph understood, because he spoke to them by an interpreter.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he turned himself away a little while, and wept: and returning, he spoke to them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And taking Simeon, and binding him in their presence, he commanded his servants to fill their sacks with wheat, and to put every man’s money again in their sacks, and to give them besides provisions for the way: and they did so.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But they having loaded their asses with the corn went their way.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And one of them opening his sack, to give his beast provender in the inn, saw the money in the sack’s mouth,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And said to his brethren: My money is given me again; behold it is in the sack. And they were astonished, and troubled, and said to one another: What is this that God hath done unto us?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they came to Jacob their father in the land of Chanaan, and they told him all things that had befallen them, saying:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The lord of the land spoke roughly to us, and took us to be spies of the country.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And we answered him: We are peaceable men, and we mean no plot.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: We are twelve brethren born of one father: one is not living, the youngest is with our father in the land of Chanaan.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said to us: Hereby shall I know that you are peaceable men: Leave one of your brethren with me, and take ye necessary provision for your houses, and go your ways,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And bring your youngest brother to me, that I may know you are not spies: and you may receive this man again, that is kept in prison: and afterwards may have leave to buy what you will.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When they had told this, they poured out their corn, and every man found his money tied in the mouth of his sack: and all being astonished together,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Their father Jacob said: You have made me to be without children: Joseph is not living, Simeon is kept in bonds, and Benjamin you will take away: all these evils are fallen upon me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Ruben answered him: Kill my two sons, if I bring him not again to thee: deliver him into my hand, and I will restore him to thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But he said: My son shall not go down with you: his brother is dead, and he is left alone: if any mischief befall him in the land to which you go, you will bring down my grey hairs with sorrow to hell.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In the mean time the famine was heavy upon all the land.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when they had eaten up all the corn, which they had brought out of Egypt, Jacob said to his sons: Go again, and buy us a little food.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Juda answered: The man declared unto us with the attestation of an oath, saying: You shall not see my face, unless you bring your youngest brother with you.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If therefore thou wilt send him with us, we will set out together, and will buy necessaries for thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But if thou wilt not, we will not go: for the man, as we have often said, declared unto us, saying: You shall not see my face without your youngest brother.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Israel said to them: You have done this for my misery, in that you told him you had also another brother.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But they answered: The man asked us in order concerning our kindred: if our father lived: if we had a brother: and we answered him regularly, according to what he demanded: could we know that he would say: Bring hither your brother with you?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Juda said to his father: Send the boy with me, that we may set forward, and may live: lest both we and our children perish.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I take the boy upon me, require him at my hand: unless I bring him again, and restore him to thee, I will be guilty of sin against thee for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If delay had not been made, we had been here again the second time.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Israel said to them: If it must needs be so, do what you will: take of the best fruits of the land in your vessels, and carry down presents to the man, a little balm, and honey, and storax, myrrh, turpentine, and almonds.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And take with you double money, and carry back what you found in your sacks, lest perhaps it was done by mistake.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And take also your brother, and go to the man.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And may my almighty God make him favourable to you: and send back with you your brother, whom he keepeth, and this Benjamin: and as for me I shall be desolate without children.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So the men took the presents, and double money, and Benjamin: and went down into Egypt, and stood before Joseph.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when he had seen them, and Benjamin with them, he commanded the steward of his house, saying: Bring in the men into the house, and kill victims, and prepare a feast: because they shall eat with me at noon.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He did as he was commanded, and brought the men into the house.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they being much afraid, said there one to another: Because of the money, which we carried back the first time in our sacks, we are brought in: that he may bring upon us a false accusation, and by violence make slaves of us and our asses.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wherefore, going up to the steward of the house, at the door,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They said: Sir, we desire thee to hear us. We came down once before to buy food:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when we had bought, and were come to the inn, we opened our sacks, and found our money in the mouths of the sacks: which we have now brought again in the same weight.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And we have brought other money besides, to buy what we want: we cannot tell who put it in our bags.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But he answered: Peace be with you, fear not: your God, and the God of your father, hath given you treasure in your sacks. For the money, which you gave me, I have for good. And he brought Simeon out to them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And having brought them into the house, he fetched water, and they washed their feet, and he gave provender to their asses.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But they made ready the presents, against Joseph came at noon: for they had heard that they should eat bread there.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Joseph came in to his house, and they offered him the presents, holding them in their hands; and they bowed down with their face to the ground.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But he courteously saluting them again, asked them, saying: Is the old man your father in health, of whom you told me? Is he yet living?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they answered: Thy servant our father, is in health; he is yet living. And bowing themselves, they made obeisance to him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Joseph lifting up his eyes, saw Benjamin, his brother by the same mother, and said: Is this your young brother, of whom you told me? And he said: God be gracious to thee, my son.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he made haste, because his heart was moved upon his brother, and tears gushed out: and going into his chamber, he wept.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when he had washed his face, coming out again, he refrained himself, and said: Set bread on the table.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when it was set on, for Joseph apart, and for his brethren apart, for the Egyptians also that ate with him apart, (for it is unlawful for the Egyptians to eat with the Hebrews, and they think such a feast profane):

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They sat before him, the firstborn according to his birthright, and the youngest according to his age. And they wondered very much;

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Taking the messes which they received of him: and the greater mess came to Benjamin, so that it exceeded by five parts. And they drank, and were merry with him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Joseph commanded the steward of his house, saying: Fill their sacks with corn, as much as they can hold: and put the money of every one in the top of his sack.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And in the mouth of the younger’s sack put my silver cup, and the price which he gave for the wheat. And it was so done.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when the morning arose, they were sent away with their asses.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when they were now departed out of the city, and had gone forward a little way: Joseph sending for the steward of his house, said: Arise, and pursue after the men: and when thou hast overtaken them, say to them: Why have you returned evil for good?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The cup which you have stolen, is that in which my lord drinketh, and in which he is wont to divine: you have done a very evil thing.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He did as he had commanded him. And having overtaken them, he spoke to them the same words.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they answered: Why doth our lord speak so, as though thy servants had committed so heinous a fact?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The money, that we found in the top of our sacks, we brought back to thee from the land of Chanaan: how then should it be that we should steal out of thy lord’s house, gold or silver?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: With whomsoever of thy servants shall be found that which thou seekest, let him die, and we will be the bondmen of my lord.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said to them: Let it be according to your sentence: with whomsoever it shall be found, let him be my servant, and you shall be blameless.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then they speedily took down their sacks to the ground, and every man opened his sack.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Which when he had searched, beginning at the eldest, and ending at the youngest, he found the cup in Benjamin’s sack.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then they rent their garments, and loading their asses again, returned into the town.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Juda at the head of his brethren went in to Joseph (for he was not yet gone out of the place) and they all together fell down before him on the ground.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said to them: Why would you do so? know you not that there is no one like me in the science of divining.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Juda said to him: What shall we answer my lord? or what shall we say, or be able justly to allege? God hath found out the iniquity of thy servants: behold, we are all bondmen to my lord, both we, and he with whom the cup was found.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Joseph answered: God forbid that I should do so: he that stole the cup, he shall be my bondman: and go you away free to your father.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Juda coming nearer, said boldly: I beseech thee, my lord, let thy servant speak a word in thy ears, and be not angry with thy servant: for after Pharao thou art.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My lord. Thou didst ask thy servants the first time: Have you a father or a brother.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And we answered thee, my lord: We have a father an old man, and a young boy, that was born in his old age; whose brother by the mother is dead; and he alone is left of his mother, and his father loveth him tenderly.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And thou saidst to thy servants: Bring him hither to me, and I will set my eyes on him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: We suggested to my lord: The boy cannot leave his father: for if he leave him, he will die.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And thou saidst to thy servants: Except your youngest brother come with you, you shall see my face no more.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Therefore when we were gone up to thy servant our father, we told him all that my lord had said.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And our father said: Go again, and buy us a little wheat.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And we said to him: We cannot go: if our youngest brother go down with us, we will set out together: otherwise, without him we dare not see the man’s face.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Whereunto he answered: You know that my wife bore me two.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: One went out, and you said: A beast devoured him; and hitherto he appeareth not.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If you take this also, and any thing befall him in the way, you will bring down my grey hairs with sorrow unto hell.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Therefore, if I shall go to thy servant, our father, and the boy be wanting, (whereas his life dependeth upon the life of him,)

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he shall see that he is not with us, he will die, and thy servants shall bring down his grey hairs with sorrow unto hell.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let me be thy proper servant, who took him into my trust, and promised, saying: If I bring him not again, I will be guilty of sin against my father for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Therefore I, thy servant, will stay instead of the boy in the service of my lord, and let the boy go up with his brethren.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For I cannot return to my father without the boy, lest I be a witness of the calamity that will oppress my father.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Joseph could no longer refrain himself before many that stood by: whereupon he commanded that all should go out, and no stranger be present at their knowing one another.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he lifted up his voice with weeping, which the Egyptians, and all the house of Pharao heard.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said to his brethren: I am Joseph: Is my father yet living? His brethren could not answer him, being struck with exceeding great fear.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said mildly to them: Come nearer to me. And when they were come near him, he said: I am Joseph, your brother, whom you sold into Egypt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Be not afraid, and let it not seem to you a hard case that you sold me into these countries: for God sent me before you into Egypt for your preservation.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For it is two years since the famine began to be upon the land, and five years more remain, wherein there can be neither ploughing nor reaping.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And God sent me before, that you may be preserved upon the earth, and may have food to live.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Not by your counsel was I sent hither, but by the will of God: who hath made me as it were a father to Pharao, and lord of his whole house, and governor in all the land of Egypt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Make haste, and go ye up to my father, and say to him: Thus saith thy son Joseph: God hath made me lord of the whole land of Egypt; come down to me, linger not.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And thou shalt dwell in the land of Gessen: and thou shalt be near me, thou and thy sons, and thy sons’ sons, thy sheep, and thy herds, and all things that thou hast.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And there I will feed thee, (for there are yet five years of famine remaining) lest both thou perish, and thy house, and all things that thou hast.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Behold, your eyes, and the eyes of my brother Benjamin, see that it is my mouth that speaketh to you.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: You shall tell my father of all my glory, and all things that you have seen in Egypt: make haste and bring him to me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And falling upon the neck of his brother Benjamin, he embraced him and wept: and Benjamin in like manner wept also on his neck.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Joseph kissed all his brethren, and wept upon every one of them: after which they were emboldened to speak to him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And it was heard, and the fame was spread abroad in the king’s court: The brethren of Joseph are come; and Pharao with all his family was glad.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he spoke to Joseph that he should give orders to his brethren, saying: Load your beasts, and go into the land of Chanaan,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And bring away from thence your father and kindred, and come to me; and I will give you all the good things of Egypt, that you may eat the marrow of the land.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Give orders also that they take wagons out of the land of Egypt, for the carriage of their children and their wives; and say: Take up your father, and make haste to come with all speed:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And leave nothing of your household stuff; for all the riches of Egypt shall be yours.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the sons of Israel did as they were bid. And Joseph gave them wagons according to Pharao’s commandment: and provisions for the way.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He ordered also to be brought out for every one of them two robes: but to Benjamin he gave three hundred pieces of silver, with five robes of the best:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Sending to his father as much money and raiment; adding besides, ten he-asses, to carry off all the riches of Egypt, and as many she-asses, carrying wheat and bread for the journey.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So he sent away his brethren, and at their departing said to them: Be not angry in the way.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they went up out of Egypt, and came into the land of Chanaan, to their father Jacob.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they told him, saying: Joseph, thy son, is living; and he is ruler in all the land of Egypt. Which when Jacob heard, he awaked as it were out of a deep sleep, yet did not believe them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They, on the other side, told the whole order of the thing. And when he saw the wagons, and all that he had sent, his spirit revived,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said: It is enough for me if Joseph, my son, be yet living: I will go and see him before I die.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Israel taking his journey, with all that he had, came to the well of the oath, and killing victims there to the God of his father Isaac,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He heard him, by a vision in the night, calling him, and saying to him: Jacob, Jacob. And he answered him: Lo, here I am.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: God said to him: I am the most mighty God of thy father; fear not, go down into Egypt, for I will make a great nation of thee there.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I will go down with thee thither, and will bring thee back again from thence: Joseph also shall put his hands upon thy eyes.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Jacob rose up from the well of the oath: and his sons took him up, with their children and wives in the wagons, which Pharao had sent to carry the old man,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And all that he had in the land of Chanaan: and he came into Egypt with all his seed;

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: His sons, and grandsons, daughters, and all his offspring together.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And these are the names of the children of Israel, that entered into Egypt, he and his children. His firstborn Ruben,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The sons of Ruben: Henoch and Phallu, and Hesron and Charmi.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The sons of Simeon: Jamuel and Jamin and Ahod, and Jachin and Sohar, and Saul, the son of a woman of Chanaan.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The sons of Levi: Gerson and Caath, and Merari.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The sons of Juda: Her and Onan, and Sela, and Phares and Zara. And Her and Onan died in the land of Chanaan. And sons were born to Phares: Hesron and Hamul.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The sons of Issachar: Thola and Phua, and Job and Semron.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The sons of Zabulon: Sared, and Elon, and Jahelel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: These are the sons of Lia, whom she bore in Mesopotamia of Syria, with Dina, his daughter. All the souls of her sons and daughters, thirty-three.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The sons of Gad: Sephion and Haggi, and Suni and Esebon, and Heri and Arodi, and Areli.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The sons of Aser: Jamne and Jesua, and Jessuri and Beria, and Sara their sister. The sons of Beria: Heber and Melchiel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: These are the sons of Zelpha, whom Laban gave to Lia, his daughter. And these she bore to Jacob, sixteen souls.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The sons of Rachel, Jacob’s wife: Joseph and Benjamin.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And sons were born to Joseph, in the land of Egypt, whom Aseneth, the daughter of Putiphare, priest of Heliopolis, bore him: Manasses and Ephraim.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The sons of Benjamin: Bela and Bechor, and Asbel and Gera, and Naaman and Echi, and Ross and Mophim, and Ophim and Ared.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: These are the sons of Rachel, whom she bore to Jacob: all the souls, fourteen.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The sons of Dan: Husim.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The sons of Nephthali: Jaziel and Guni, and Jeser and Sallem.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: These are the sons of Bala, whom Laban gave to Rachel, his daughter: and these she bore to Jacob: all the souls, seven.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: All the souls that went with Jacob into Egypt, and that came out of his thigh, besides his sons’ wives, sixty-six.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the sons of Joseph, that were born to him in the land of Egypt, two souls. All the souls of the house of Jacob, that entered into Egypt, were seventy.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he sent Juda before him to Joseph, to tell him; and that he should meet him in Gessen.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when he was come thither, Joseph made ready his chariot, and went up to meet his father in the same place: and seeing him, he fell upon his neck, and embracing him, wept.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the father said to Joseph: Now shall I die with joy, because I have seen thy face, and leave thee alive.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Joseph said to his brethren, and to all his father’s house: I will go up, and will tell Pharao, and will say to him: My brethren, and my father’s house, that were in the land of Chanaan, are come to me:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the men are shepherds, and their occupation is to feed cattle; their flocks, and herds, and all they have, they have brought with them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when he shall call you, and shall say: What is your occupation?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: You shall answer: We, thy servants, are shepherds, from our infancy until now, both we and our fathers. And this you shall say, that you may dwell in the land of Gessen, because the Egyptians have all shepherds in abomination.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Joseph went in and told Pharao, saying: My father and brethren, their sheep and their herds, and all that they possess, are come out of the land of Chanaan: and behold they stay in the land of Gessen.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Five men also, the last of his brethren, he presented before the king:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he asked them: What is your occupation? They answered: We, thy servants, are shepherds, both we and our fathers.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: We are come to sojourn in thy land, because there is no grass for the flocks of thy servants, the famine being very grievous in the land of Chanaan: and we pray thee to give orders that we thy servants may be in the land of Gessen.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The king therefore said to Joseph: Thy father and thy brethren are come to thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The land of Egypt is before thee: and make them dwell in the best place, and give them the land of Gessen. And if thou knowest that there are industrious men among them, make them rulers over my cattle.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: After this Joseph brought in his father to the king, and presented him before him: and he blessed him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And being asked by him: How many are the days of the years of thy life?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He answered: The days of my pilgrimage are a hundred and thirty years, few, and evil, and they are not come up to the days of the pilgrimage of my fathers.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And blessing the king, he went out.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Joseph gave a possession to his father and his brethren in Egypt, in the best place of the land, in Ramesses, as Pharao had commanded.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he nourished them, and all his father’s house, allowing food to every one.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For in the whole world there was want of bread, and a famine had oppressed the land, more especially of Egypt and Chanaan;

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Out of which he gathered up all the money for the corn which they bought, and brought it in to the king’s treasure.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when the buyers wanted money, all Egypt came to Joseph, saying: Give us bread: why should we die in thy presence, having now no money?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he answered them: Bring me your cattle, and for them I will give you food, if you have no money.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when they had brought them, he gave them food in exchange for their horses, and sheep, and oxen, and asses: and he maintained them that year for the exchange of their cattle.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they came the second year, and said to him: We will not hide from our lord, how that our money is spent, and our cattle also are gone: neither art thou ignorant that we have nothing now left but our bodies and our lands.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Why therefore shall we die before thy eyes? we will be thine, both we and our lands: buy us to be the king’s servants, and give us seed, lest for want of tillers the land be turned into a wilderness.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So Joseph bought all the land of Egypt, every man selling his possessions, because of the greatness of the famine. And he brought it into Pharao’s hands:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And all its people from one end of the borders of Egypt, even to the other end thereof,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Except the land of the priests, which had been given them by the king: to whom also a certain allowance of food was given out of the public stores, and therefore they were not forced to sell their possessions.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Joseph said to the people: Behold, as you see, both you and your lands belong to Pharao; take seed and sow the fields,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That you may have corn. The fifth part you shall give to the king; the other four you shall have for seed, and for food for your families and children.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they answered: our life is in thy hand; only let my lord look favourably upon us, and we will gladly serve the king.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: From that time unto this day, in the whole land of Egypt, the fifth part is paid to the kings, and it is become as a law, except the land of the priests, which was free from this covenant.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So Israel dwelt in Egypt, that is, in the land of Gessen, and possessed it; and grew, and was multiplied exceedingly.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he lived in it seventeen years: and all the days of his life came to a hundred and forty-seven years.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when he saw that the day of his death drew nigh, he called his son Joseph, and said to him: If I have found favour in thy sight, put thy hand under my thigh; and thou shalt shew me this kindness and truth, not to bury me in Egypt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But I will sleep with my fathers, and thou shalt take me away out of this land, and bury me in the burying place of my ancestors. And Joseph answered him: I will do what thou hast commanded.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said: Swear then to me. And as he was swearing, Israel adored God, turning to the bed’s head.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: After these things, it was told Joseph that his father was sick; and he set out to go to him, taking his two sons Manasses and Ephraim.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And it was told the old man: Behold thy son Joseph cometh to thee. And being strengthened, he sat on his bed.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when Joseph was come in to him, he said: God almighty appeared to me at Luza, which is in the land of Chanaan, and he blessed me,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And said: I will cause thee to increase and multiply, and I will make of thee a multitude of people: and I will give this land to thee, and to thy seed after thee for an everlasting possession.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So thy two sons, who were born to thee in the land of Egypt before I came hither to thee, shall be mine: Ephraim and Manasses shall be reputed to me as Ruben and Simeon.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But the rest whom thou shalt have after them, shall be thine, and shall be called by the name of their brethren in their possessions.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For, when I came out of Mesopotamia, Rachel died from me in the land of Chanaan in the very journey, and it was spring time: and I was going to Ephrata, and I buried her near the way of Ephrata, which by another name is called Bethlehem.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then seeing his sons, he said to him: Who are these?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He answered: They are my sons, whom God hath given me in this place. And he said: Bring them to me, that I may bless them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For Israel’s eyes were dim by reason of his great age, and he could not see clearly. And when they were brought to him, he kissed and embraced them,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And said to his son: I am not deprived of seeing thee; moreover God hath shewn me thy seed.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when Joseph had taken them from his father’s lap, he bowed down with his face to the ground.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he set Ephraim on his right hand, that is, towards the left hand of Israel; but Manasses on his left hand, to wit, towards his father’s right hand, and brought them near to him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But he, stretching forth his right hand, put it upon the head of Ephraim, the younger brother; and the left upon the head of Manasses, who was the elder, changing his hands.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner
